name: Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop
name_original: Montañas Vacías – circular desde Guadalaviar por el Nacimiento del Río Tajo
description: Cycle 31.6 miles (50.9 km) through Spain's remote Empty Mountains, exploring the Tajo River source and diverse landscapes.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Guadalaviar traverse a remote region characterized by diverse landscapes. The area features expansive high-elevation plateaus, dense pine and juniper forests, and deep canyons, including the Guadalaviar River Canyon. It is situated within or borders the Serranía de Cuenca Natural Park, offering varied terrain for cycling. The Source of the River Tagus is also a notable natural attraction within the region. Best touring cycling routes around Guadalaviar The most popular touring cycling route is Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop, a 31.6 miles (50.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 32 minutes to complete. This route showcases the region's diverse terrain, including remote wilderness. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo, a moderate 19.1 miles (30.7 km) path. This route features gravel roads and passes near the Source of the River Tagus. Local touring cyclists also love the Puerto Muela de San Juan – Griegos loop from Griegos, a 29.5 miles (47.5 km) trail leading through high-elevation areas, often completed in about 4 hours 59 minutes. Touring cycling around Guadalaviar is defined by remote wilderness, high-elevation plateaus, and pine and juniper forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, with a focus on challenging routes. The routes in Guadalaviar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 7 reviews. More than 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadalaviar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Guadalaviar?
A: Guadalaviar's touring cycling routes predominantly feature challenging terrain, including gravel forest roads and doubletracks. Many segments are unpaved and difficult, sometimes requiring you to push your bike. A gravel bike with wide wheels or a mountain bike is highly recommended for navigating the region's diverse landscapes, which include high-elevation plateaus, dense pine and juniper forests, and deep canyons.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Guadalaviar?
A: Yes, Guadalaviar offers several circular touring cycling routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e956564587/empty-mountains-guadalaviar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is a difficult 31.6-mile (50.9 km) trail showcasing the region's remote wilderness. Another popular choice is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1094016335/puente-de-las-tres-provincias-circular-gravel-from-the-nacimiento-del-rio-tajo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 19.1-mile (30.7 km) path that passes near the Source of the River Tagus.

Q: What natural attractions can I discover along the touring cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can cycle near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4740317" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Cuervo River&lt;/a&gt;, witness the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4740327" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuervo River Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5400567" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Molino Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, or explore the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4542067" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moricacho of the Gorge&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4542097" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barranco Mingo Marco&lt;/a&gt;. The Guadalaviar River Canyon itself offers a nature trail with views of an old dam and rich flora. Many routes also traverse the Serranía de Cuenca Natural Park.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by the Source of the River Tagus?
A: Yes, the Source of the River Tagus is a significant natural attraction in the region. The moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38161265" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalaviar – Source of the River Tagus loop from Guadalaviar&lt;/a&gt;, a 21.9 km (13.6 miles) route, specifically takes you to this area, known for its crystal-clear turquoise waters. You can also find routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1094016335/puente-de-las-tres-provincias-circular-gravel-from-the-nacimiento-del-rio-tajo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo&lt;/a&gt; that pass nearby.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Guadalaviar?
A: The touring cycling routes in Guadalaviar are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 7 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the profound sense of solitude offered by the remote wilderness and expansive high-elevation plateaus, as well as the stunning natural beauty of the pine and juniper forests and deep canyons.

Q: Are there any less strenuous touring cycling routes for beginners or those seeking an easier ride?
A: While Guadalaviar is known for its challenging routes, there are a few moderate options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1094016335/puente-de-las-tres-provincias-circular-gravel-from-the-nacimiento-del-rio-tajo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 19.1-mile (30.7 km) route, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38161265" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalaviar – Source of the River Tagus loop from Guadalaviar&lt;/a&gt; is another moderate 13.6-mile (21.9 km) option. These routes offer a less demanding experience compared to the region's more difficult trails.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while cycling in Guadalaviar?
A: Guadalaviar is rich in wildlife, offering a 'European safari park' experience. Cyclists often report sightings of deer, wild boar, and various vultures. The area is home to vulture colonies, including griffon vultures, golden eagles, Egyptian vultures, and bearded vultures, particularly visible along the Guadalaviar Nature Trail.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Guadalaviar?
A: There are over 10 touring cycling routes available around Guadalaviar, offering a variety of experiences through its remote wilderness, high-elevation plateaus, and dense forests. The majority of these routes are classified as difficult, catering to experienced touring cyclists.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the high-elevation areas around Guadalaviar?
A: Yes, many routes in Guadalaviar traverse high-elevation plateaus. For example, the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31845556" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Muela de San Juan – Griegos loop from Griegos&lt;/a&gt; is a 29.5-mile (47.5 km) trail that leads through significant high-elevation areas, offering expansive views and challenging climbs.

Q: What is the typical duration for a touring cycling route in this region?
A: The duration of touring cycling routes in Guadalaviar varies depending on the distance and difficulty. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e956564587/empty-mountains-guadalaviar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 31.6-mile (50.9 km) difficult route, typically takes around 4 hours and 32 minutes to complete. Shorter, moderate routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1094016335/puente-de-las-tres-provincias-circular-gravel-from-the-nacimiento-del-rio-tajo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in about 2 hours and 30 minutes.

Q: Are there any routes that start directly from Guadalaviar?
A: Yes, several routes offer convenient starting points directly from Guadalaviar. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31845194" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Portillo – Guadalaviar loop from Guadalaviar&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 15.3-mile (24.5 km) trail that begins and ends in the town. Another option is the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38161265" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalaviar – Source of the River Tagus loop from Guadalaviar&lt;/a&gt;, also starting from Guadalaviar.
tips:
The official source of water is located at Fuente de García, in the Montes Universales, within the municipality of Frías de Albarracín (Teruel). It is located at an altitude of approximately 1,590 meters, surrounded by pine forests and high mountain meadows.
Access is very easy and well signposted. Take the A-1704 highway, which connects Frías de Albarracín with Huélamo (Cuenca). From the highway, a paved detour of approximately 2 km takes you directly to the recreational area and the monument. There is free parking next to the monument.
El Monolito forest shelter. Ideal place to spend the night indoors. There are no services other than a sleeping room and a common space to cook. Keep it clean and don't leave trash.
It is a beautiful picnic area with wooden tables surrounded by trees next to the Tagus River and the border between the provinces of Cuenca and Teruel.

La Fuente Coveta is a picnic area and recreational area located in a wonderful setting with amazing tranquility. Located between the towns of Griegos and Guadalaviar and a few meters from the GR 10.1 trail, it is an ideal place to stop on your route and enjoy this beautiful setting.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Guadalaviar. You should be able to find parking within the village, which serves as a convenient base for this circular tour.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route primarily features gravel forest roads and doubletracks, with some paved sections. Be prepared for unpaved and potentially difficult segments that might require you to push your bike. The initial sections near the Tajo headwaters are described as quite complicated, with narrow and hilly paths.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll pass by several notable spots, including the symbolic Source of the River Tagus. Other highlights include the Forest House Refuge, a Forest Shelter and Picnic Area by the Tagus River, and another Picnic Area.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is rated as difficult. It involves significant elevation gains and is part of the larger Montañas Vacías network, known for its challenging nature. The remoteness and considerable climbing make it unsuitable for beginners. A gravel bike with wide wheels or a mountain bike is recommended.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While the route can be enjoyed in warmer months, be aware that even in summer, mornings can be very cold, requiring warm clothing. The region offers stunning scenery year-round, but spring and autumn might provide more moderate temperatures for cycling.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route, especially within Serranía de Cuenca Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route or access the natural areas it traverses, including the Serranía de Cuenca Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning wildlife and protected environments.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Given the route's remote nature and the abundance of wildlife, it's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash to protect both your pet and the local fauna. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and capable of handling the distance and terrain. Check local park regulations for any specific restrictions.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife and scenery can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: You'll experience incredibly varied and spectacular landscapes, from deep river canyons with crystal-clear turquoise waters to high-elevation plateaus and dense forests. The area is rich in wildlife, often described as a "European safari park," with common sightings of deer, wild boar, ibex, hares, foxes, and vultures. The skies are also among the cleanest in Spain.

faqs.question: Are there opportunities for wild swimming along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the section along the Tajo River, particularly near its source, offers numerous opportunities for wild swimming in its crystal-clear turquoise waters. It's a refreshing highlight, especially during warmer weather.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is part of the larger MontañasVacías bikepacking network (covering about 70% of this specific route). It also intersects with sections of the Camino Natural del Tajo (28%) and other local trails like [CIMA TE12] El Portillo (11%) and Rubiales - Orihuela del Tremedal (9%).

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging cycling route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and remoteness, you should bring a well-maintained gravel or mountain bike, plenty of water, and sufficient food. Resupply points are infrequent, with up to 70 kilometers between stops in some sections. Warm clothing, including winter gloves and jackets, is essential even in summer due to cold mornings. A repair kit and navigation tools are also highly recommended.

waypoints.name: Forest Shelter and Picnic Area by the Tagus River
